skill.name: faceless-explainer
skill.description: Create faceless explainer videos from text briefs, notes, scripts, or articles using HyperFrames. The skill plans the story, visual system, invented graphics, audio, captions, transitions, validation, and final render without website capture.
skill.summary: Turn text into a narrated HyperFrames faceless explainer video.
skill.author: heygen-com

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported 1059 issues and a critical heuristic, but review found no prompt injection, credential exfiltration, or malicious intent. Most alerts are markdown backticks, relative documentation paths, color parsing, or RegExp.exec false positives. Confirmed risks are legitimate HyperFrames workflow privileges: local scripts, project filesystem writes, an environment-controlled audio engine override, and generated HTML that loads GSAP from a CDN.

medium_findings.title: Local Subprocess Invocation for Audio Pipeline
medium_findings.description: The audio adapter imports child_process and uses spawnSync to run a Node-based shared media audio engine. This is expected for narration, BGM, and SFX generation, and arguments are passed as an array without shell interpolation. It still creates a local command execution surface when users run the workflow, especially because HF_MEDIA_ENGINE can override the engine path.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess call is real and semantically tied to media generation. Confidence is below very high because it appears legitimate and I did not find shell interpolation or evidence of malicious command construction.

medium_findings.title: Project Filesystem Modification by Workflow Scripts
medium_findings.description: Several scripts write generated project artifacts such as frame.md, caption HTML, caption metadata, audio metadata, and index.html. This is necessary for HyperFrames rendering, but users should run it only in the intended video project directory because the scripts accept project paths from CLI arguments.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are directly present and affect local project files. Confidence is medium-high because this is expected behavior for a video rendering skill, not evidence of destructive intent.

medium_findings.title: Generated HTML Loads External GSAP CDN Script
medium_findings.description: The generated index and fallback captions HTML include GSAP from jsDelivr. This is a normal rendering dependency, but it introduces a runtime network and supply-chain dependency unless the environment pins or vendors the asset.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hardcoded CDN URL is visible in generated HTML templates. It is not exfiltration, but the external runtime dependency is clear.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Command Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Many external command alerts come from markdown code formatting and workflow instructions, not hidden Ruby backtick execution. The cited lines describe commands users may run as part of the HyperFrames workflow.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files are markdown documentation, and the surrounding text explains user-facing workflow commands or examples. I did not find Ruby code at these locations.

low_findings.title: Regex exec Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The script findings labeled as Python exec or process exec are RegExp.exec calls used for parsing headings, colors, and transition HTML. These do not execute code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines call regular expression exec methods and parse strings. There is no dynamic evaluation or command execution at these locations.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Alerts Are Documentation Relative Paths
low_findings.description: Several path traversal alerts refer to documentation links such as ../hyperframes-core and ../hyperframes-animation. These are intended skill reference paths, not code that reads arbitrary parent directories.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are markdown references to neighboring HyperFrames skill documentation. They do not perform filesystem reads by themselves.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Color and Design Vocabulary
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts are caused by terms such as hex colors, chroma, blur, and visual design language. I did not find hashing, encryption, or cryptographic verification code in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited script parses color hex values and design tokens, while the reference line discusses palette usage. The semantic context is visual design, not cryptography.

dangerous_patterns.title: child_process.spawnSync Usage
dangerous_patterns.description: spawnSync runs a Node media engine for audio work. It is not shell-based, but it should remain limited to trusted local workflow execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The child_process import and spawnSync call are direct evidence. The implementation uses an argument array, which lowers injection risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment-Controlled Engine Path
dangerous_patterns.description: HF_MEDIA_ENGINE can replace the default shared audio engine path. This is useful for local configuration but should not be set from untrusted input.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable is directly read and used to choose the engine path. Risk depends on who controls the runtime environment.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hardcoded External Script URL
dangerous_patterns.description: Generated compositions reference GSAP on jsDelivr. This is a known dependency, not covert networking, but marketplace users should understand the runtime network behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The CDN URL is directly embedded in generated HTML templates. No evidence shows secret data being sent to it.

content.value_statement: Turning dense notes into a polished video is slow and hard to structure. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through a HyperFrames workflow for text-based faceless explainers.
actual_capabilities: Turns articles, notes, briefs, or scripts into a faceless explainer video plan.
Creates a HyperFrames project flow with brief, design system, storyboard, script, audio, frames, validation, and render steps.
Uses local references for story design, composition, visual design, and motion language.
Provides scripts for frame preset adoption, audio metadata, captions, transitions, and index assembly.
Supports landscape, portrait, and square video formats through storyboard settings.
Coordinates one frame worker per storyboard frame for invented typography, diagrams, abstract graphics, and data visualization.
limitations: It is not for product launch videos, website tours, pull request explainers, or captioning existing footage.
It requires HyperFrames tooling and local project execution to produce a final MP4.
It does not capture websites or collect real asset inventories.
Audio and music behavior depends on HeyGen access or local fallback engines.

use_cases.title: Teach a complex topic
use_cases.description: Convert lesson notes or an article into a narrated visual explainer with a clear teaching arc.

use_cases.title: Publish social explainers
use_cases.description: Create short portrait or square videos that explain a concept without filming people or capturing websites.

use_cases.title: Explain internal knowledge
use_cases.description: Turn process notes, policy summaries, or research briefs into structured training videos.

prompt_templates.title: Start from notes
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 60 second faceless explainer from these notes. Use a clear audience, simple narration, and invented visuals only: [paste notes].

prompt_templates.title: Adapt an article
prompt_templates.prompt: Turn this article into a 90 second portrait faceless explainer. Reorder the ideas for clarity and avoid following the article paragraph order: [paste article].

prompt_templates.title: Use a fixed script
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a HyperFrames faceless explainer using this script verbatim. Segment it into frames, design invented visuals, and prepare validation steps: [paste script].

prompt_templates.title: Produce a full workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a complete faceless explainer workflow for this topic, including brief, storyboard, narration, visual direction, frame dispatch plan, audio, captions, validation, and render steps: [topic and constraints].

output_examples.input: A briefing note about why compound interest accelerates over time.
output_examples.output: A six-frame teaching plan with a snowball metaphor, concise narration, a diagram build, captions, transition notes, and render validation steps.

output_examples.input: A policy summary explaining a new internal approval process.
output_examples.output: A process explainer with ordered steps, invented flow diagrams, short voiceover lines, timing guidance, and a final HyperFrames render path.

output_examples.input: A science article about ocean acidification for a general audience.
output_examples.output: A narrative explainer that opens with stakes, introduces the core mechanism, visualizes cause and effect, and closes with a clear takeaway.

best_practices: Lock the audience, angle, length, aspect ratio, and language before writing the storyboard.
Use the source text as information, then rebuild the story around the clearest teaching sequence.
Run HyperFrames lint, validate, inspect, and snapshot checks before rendering the final MP4.
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for a product promo, website capture, pull request video, or existing footage captioning task.
Do not paraphrase the source paragraph by paragraph when a stronger teaching order is possible.
Do not invent captured assets or website screenshots for a faceless explainer.

faq.question: What does faceless mean in this skill?
faq.answer: Faceless means the video uses invented visuals such as typography, diagrams, abstract graphics, and data visualization instead of filmed people or captured websites.

faq.question: Can it use a website as the source?
faq.answer: No. Use the website-to-video workflow for site tours and product-launch-video for product promos based on URLs.

faq.question: Can I provide my own script?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill can segment a pasted script into frame-sized narration, including a verbatim mode when the words must not change.

faq.question: Does it generate the final video file?
faq.answer: It guides HyperFrames project creation, validation, and rendering. A local render step creates the final MP4 after review approval.

faq.question: What visuals does it create?
faq.answer: It plans and builds typography, diagrams, abstract graphics, data visualization, and motion treatments matched to the explainer story.

faq.question: What tools are required?
faq.answer: A working HyperFrames setup is required. Audio may use HeyGen services when signed in or local fallback behavior when available.
